claim: The tested Fe-Mn-Al-C grades have Al content ranging from 9 to 12 wt%, Mn levels of 10, 20, or 30 wt%, C content of 0.4 to 1 wt%, with balance Fe.
evidence_text: Table 1 – Chemical composition ranges of the tested Fe–Mn–Al–C grades. Al 9–12 Mn 10–20–30 C 0.4–1 Fe Bal.

claim: The 9Al-10Mn-1C steel exhibits Vickers hardness of 273 HV in hot-rolled condition and 439 HV after tempering at 600 °C, indicating a hardening effect due to discontinuous precipitation.
evidence_text: Example of hardening effects of discontinuous precipitation. Composition (wt%) 9Al-10Mn-1C Hardness [HV] Hot-rolled 273, 600 °C — Tempered 439

claim: For alloys with 1% C, spinodal decomposition was triggered at the expense of cellular transformation, influenced by the κ-carbide driving force.
evidence_text: For 1% C spinodal decomposition was triggered at the expense of cellular transformation, as far as austenite stability is influenced by the κ-carbide driving force as well.

claim: SEM micrographs (Fig. 2) reveal lamellar structures consisting of γ + κ-carbides formed by discontinuous precipitation at 600 °C in 9Al-10Mn-0.4C and 9Al-10Mn-1C.
evidence_text: Discontinuous precipitation in - + κ-carbides lamellae at 600 ◦C in: (a) 9Al-10Mn-0.4C; (b) 9Al-10Mn-1C.

claim: Four identified austenite decomposition reactions are: discontinuous precipitation (γ → γ0 + κ), precipitation transformation (γ → γ0 + κ), cellular transformation (γ → α + κ or γ → α + κ + γ0), and spinodal decomposition (γ → γ0 + γ' → γ0 + L12 → γ0 + L12/E21).
evidence_text: the different austenite transformations: discontinuous precipitation (γ → γ0 + κ), precipitation transformation (γ → γ0 + κ), cellular transformation (γ → α + κ or γ → α + κ + γ0) and spinodal decomposition (γ → γ0 + γ' → γ0 + L12 → γ0 + L12/E21).

claim: Intergranular κ-carbides forming from discontinuous precipitation and cellular transformation induce serious brittleness and affect structural stability, strength, and ductility during hot-working, cold-working, and room temperature loading.
evidence_text: Due to their morphology and distribution along boundaries/phase interfaces these intergranular κ-carbides induce serious brittleness. They affect material structural stability, strength and ductility both during hot-working, cold-working and under nominal loading conditions at room temperature.

claim: Spinodal decomposition generates intragranular nano-sized cube-shaped κ-carbides within γ-phase, which are associated with strengthening effects.
evidence_text: Spinodal decomposition, however, generates intragranular nano-sized cube-shaped κ-carbides within the γ-phase, and they are usually associated with strengthening effects.

claim: The degree of carbon supersaturation in austenite is suspected to play a crucial role in triggering the formation of intragranular κ-carbides during fast cooling.
evidence_text: The degree of carbon supersaturation in austenite has been suspected of playing a crucial role in triggering the formation of intragranular κ-carbides during fast cooling.

claim: After tempering at 600 °C, 9Al-10Mn-0.4C exhibited partial discontinuous precipitation, while 9Al-10Mn-1C showed almost complete transformation of austenite to γ+κ lamellar structures.
evidence_text: At 9Al-10Mn-0.4C, γ grains underwent only partial discontinuous precipitation (Fig. 1(a)), whereas at 9Al-10Mn-1C almost all the austenite volume was transformed into the above-mentioned lamellar structure, where it might be possible that some δ-phase is also present.

claim: In 9Al-10Mn-1C tempered at 600 °C, most austenite transformed via discontinuous precipitation into fine γ+κ lamellae, while untransformed austenite regions contained nano-sized κ-carbides as indicated by EDS.
evidence_text: Whereas, at 9Al-10Mn-1C most of the austenite is transformed through the discontinuous precipitation reaction (Fig. 2(b)). … Moreover, in the 9Al-10Mn-1C, the non-discontinuous precipitated austenite seems to have featured nano-sized κ-carbide precipitation, as suggested by EDS (Table 3).

claim: In 12Al-10Mn-0.4C and 12Al-10Mn-1C tempered at 600 °C, austenite transformation produced a matrix of carbon-depleted γ regions with globular κ-carbides instead of γ+κ lamellae, accompanied by strong κ-carbide precipitation along δ/γ boundaries.
evidence_text: At 12% Al the reactions involved the whole γ volume (Fig. 3(a)). For example, at 12Al-10Mn-0.4C … Moreover, it did not develop γ+κ-carbide lamellar structures, but an austenite matrix only featured by carbon-depleted γ regions with globular κ-carbides. This austenite transformation was also accompanied by strong κ-carbide precipitation along δ/γ boundaries and poor precipitation of κ-carbides within ferrite.

claim: In 12Al-10Mn-1C tempered at 600 °C, a precipitation transformation generated a complex microstructure with γ+κ lamellae and alternating bands of two different austenite grades, raising hardness from 401 HV (hot-rolled) to 420 HV.
evidence_text: In medium Mn with a high Al content of 12% (Fig. 4) tempered at 600 °C, γ passed through a more complex random-like transformation, not as ordered and defined as discontinuous precipitation, and in this case it was been linked to slight hardening effects (Table 4). … Not only γ+κ lamellae, but also alternated bands of what seem to be two different austenite grades are observable.

claim: At 800 °C, 9Al-10Mn-1C underwent both cellular transformation (producing κ+α+γ lamellae) and spinodal decomposition, with hardness rising from 273 HV (hot-rolled) to 356 HV.
evidence_text: Only the alloys tempered at 800 °C underwent cellular transformation and/or spinodal decomposition. For high Al and C with medium Mn, austenite underwent both spinodal decomposition and cellular transformation. This microstructure is therefore characterised by wide-spread κ+α+γ lamellar structures, derived from γ cellular transformation, thin band-shaped δ-ferrite grains and scattered regions of spinodal decomposed γ (Fig. 5(a)), followed by a significant increase in hardness (Table 5).

claim: In 12Al-20Mn-1C tempered at 800 °C, cellular transformation produced wide, thick lath-like κ-carbides in κ+α+γ lamellae, and significant hardening occurred despite high inter-lamellar spacing.
evidence_text: At 12% aluminium the κ-carbide lath-like strips look wider and thicker than at 9% Al. In these cases, even though the inter-lamellar spacing is high the hardening effects were consistent as shown in Table 5.

claim: Nano-sized κ-carbides from spinodal decomposition exhibited morphology variations: thin acicular in 9Al-10Mn-1C (medium-dense grid), densest and thickest cuboidal in 12Al-20Mn-1C, and finest acicular with least dense configuration in 12Al-30Mn-1C.
evidence_text: In 9Al-10Mn-1C, κ-carbides agglomerated into thin acicular morphologies in a relatively medium-dense grid (Fig. 8(a), (b)). In 12Al-20Mn-1C, κ-carbides presented the densest and thickest configuration with proper cuboidal morphology (Fig. 8(c), (d)). In 12Al-30Mn-1C, κ-carbides agglomerated again into acicular-like precipitates, but with the finest and less dense configuration (Fig. 8(e), (f)).

claim: Spinodal decomposition in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels generates intragranular nano-sized cube-shaped κ-carbides that are associated with strengthening effects.
evidence_text: Spinodal decomposition, however, generates intragranular nano-sized cube-shaped κ-carbides within the γ-phase [1,37–40], and they are usually associated with strengthening effects [1,37].

claim: Carbon and aluminium concentrations influence κ-carbide formation by affecting the chemical driving force and coherency strain energy between κ-carbides and the γ matrix.
evidence_text: Carbon and aluminium concentrations mainly affect κ-carbide formation [1,38–41]. Al and C amounts influence the chemical driving force and the coherency strain energy between κ-carbides and γ, which are essential for γ decomposition [37,43].

claim: Spinodal decomposition has been reported to occur during quenching, and the degree of carbon supersaturation in austenite is suspected to trigger intragranular κ-carbide formation during fast cooling.
evidence_text: some works have also reported spinodal decomposition occurring during quenching [40,41,43,46]. The degree of carbon supersaturation in austenite has been suspected of playing a crucial role in triggering the formation of intragranular κ-carbides during fast cooling [43].

claim: κ-carbides presented the densest and thickest configuration with proper cuboidal morphology (Fig. 8(c), (d)).
evidence_text: In 12Al-20Mn-1C, κ-carbides presented the densest and thickest configuration with proper cuboidal morphology (Fig. 8(c), (d)).

claim: κ-carbides agglomerated again into acicular-like precipitates, but with the finest and less dense configuration (Fig. 8(e), (f)).
evidence_text: In 12Al-30Mn-1C, κ-carbides agglomerated again into acicular-like precipitates, but with the finest and less dense configuration (Fig. 8(e), (f)).

claim: EDS analysis gave mean compositions of δ phase: Al 8.4 wt%, Mn 8.3 wt%; γ phase: Al 7.4 wt%, Mn 11.4 wt%; κ phase: Al 9.4 wt%, Mn 12.3 wt% (Table 6).
evidence_text: Table 6 – EDS for Al and Mn in 9Al-10Mn-1C tempered at 800 °C.

claim: Austenite decomposition occurred during tempering at 600 °C but not at 800 °C, consistent with no decrease in austenite fraction at 800 °C relative to 1150 °C in the phase volume fraction diagram (Fig. 9(a)).
evidence_text: the 9Al-10Mn-0.4C alloy underwent austenite decomposition during tempering at 600 °C (Fig. 1(a)), but not at 800 °C even though kinetics should have been faster. However, this is not incoherent. In fact, the non-transforming behaviour of austenite reflects the respective phase volume fraction diagram (Fig. 9(a)).

claim: Discontinuous precipitation at 600 °C was associated with a drop of γ volume fraction from 1150 °C to 600 °C (Fig. 9(a)), while γ fraction gradients in 9Al-10Mn-1C at both 600 °C and 800 °C triggered austenite decomposition (Fig. 9(b)).
evidence_text: Discontinuous precipitation took place in 9Al-10Mn-0.4C at 600 °C (Fig. 1(a)). Actually, in its phase diagram the drop of γ volume fraction from 1150 °C to 600 °C (Fig. 9(a)) was pointed out. Moreover, the γ fraction gradient was also detected in 9Al-10Mn-1C at 600 °C and 800 °C (Fig. 9(b)).

claim: Lower driving force caused by lower annealing temperature at 600 °C allowed grain boundary migration of forming lamellar phase rather than completing lamellae separation.
evidence_text: During the discontinuous precipitation, a lower driving force, caused by a lower annealing temperature at 600 °C allowed grain boundary migration of the forming lamellar phase rather than completing lamellae separation.

claim: Manganese and carbon concentrations were not high enough to stabilize the γ phase against its own transformation, with boundary conditions of 10% Mn and thermal energy introduced at 600 °C enabling decomposition.
evidence_text: The manganese and carbon concentrations were not high enough to stabilize the γ phase against its own transformation. This austenite evolution was granted by the following boundary conditions: 10% of manganese. Thermal energy introduced at 600 °C.

claim: At 800 °C, 9Al-10Mn-1C underwent cellular transformation for most of the austenite fraction and spinodal decomposition for the remainder, producing δ + α + residual γ lamellar phases (Fig. 6, Table 6).
evidence_text: At an 800 °C annealing temperature, different equilibrium states are identified. Such boundary conditions developed at 9Al-10Mn-1C cellular transformation for most of the austenite fraction and spinodal decomposition for the remainder. Cellular transformation generated δ + α + residual-γ lamellar phases (Fig. 6, Table 6).

claim: Vickers hardness of 9Al-10Mn-1C increased from 273 HV (hot-rolled) to 439 HV at 600 °C tempered, then dropped to 356 HV at 800 °C; 9Al-20Mn-1C: 233 to 359 HV (600 °C) and 249 HV (800 °C); 9Al-30Mn-1C: 216 to 330 HV (600 °C) and 238 HV (800 °C) (Table 8).
evidence_text: Table 8 – A comparison among the hardening effects in 9Al-xMn-1C at 600 °C and 800 °C tempering.

claim: In 9Al-20Mn-1C tempered at 600 °C, δ + κ phases are present along grain boundaries with continuity to δ grains (Fig. 10(a), 11), while in 9Al-30Mn-1C only κ-carbides are detected (Fig. 10(b)); both feature parallel striations in austenite grains (Fig. 10).
evidence_text: They are probably δ + κ in 9Al-20Mn-1C (since there seems to be a continuity with δ grains) (Fig. 10(a) and 11) and only κ-carbides in 9Al-30Mn-1C (Fig. 10(b)). It is interesting that in both cases the austenite featured parallel striations with different directions in each grain (Fig. 10).

claim: For 9Al-20Mn-1C and 9Al-30Mn-1C, the austenite fraction is higher at 800 °C than at 1150 °C (Fig. 12(a),(b)), whereas for 12Al-20Mn-1C and 12Al-30Mn-1C the austenite fraction is lower at 800 °C than at 1150 °C (Fig. 12(c),(d)), explaining the enhanced driving force for decomposition in the 12Al alloys.
evidence_text: For example, for the 9Al-20/30Mn-1C composition, the austenite fraction is higher (which may indicate more stability) at 800 °C than 1150 °C (Fig. 12(a), (b)). On the contrary, for 12Al-20/30Mn-1C the situation was reversed (Fig. 12(c), (d)).

claim: 12Al-10Mn-1C tempered at 800 °C had full cellular transformation into δ + κ-carbide coarse lamellae with high inter-lamellar spacing and a hardness of 399 HV, whereas 9Al-10Mn-1C tempered at 600 °C had fine lamellae with narrow inter-lamellar spacing and a hardness of 439 HV (Fig. 5(b), Table 9).
evidence_text: At 12Al-10Mn-1C austenite underwent full cellular transformation into δ + κ-carbide coarse lamellae, featuring high inter-lamellar spacing (Fig. 5(b)) ... 9Al-10Mn-1C alloys treated at 600 °C, with fine lamellae featuring short inter-lamellar spacing (Fig. 2(b)) showed significant hardening effects (Table 9).

claim: As Mn content increases, spinodal decomposition becomes more significant at the expense of cellular transformation; cellular fractions predominated in 9Al-10Mn-1C and 12Al-20Mn-1C, whereas only spinodal decomposition occurred in 12Al-30Mn-1C at 800 °C (Fig. 13).
evidence_text: cellular transformed fractions were predominant over the spinodal decomposed ones, due to lower Mn%-γ stability, at 9Al-10Mn-1C (Fig. 13(a)) and 12Al-20Mn-1C (Fig. 13(b)) grades; instead, at 12Al-30Mn-1C only spinodal decomposition has involved the entire austenitic volume (Fig. 13(c)).

claim: Some grains feature a mixed structure with δ ferrite branches extending from δ + κ lamellar grains into spinodally decomposed γ grains (Fig. 14).
evidence_text: In 12Al-20Mn-1C tempered at 800 °C (Fig. 5(e)) it is interesting to note that some grains feature a mixed structure: δ ferrite branches (linked to δ + κ-carbide lamellar grains) are extended inside spinodal decomposed γ grains (Fig. 14).

claim: The best microstructure was obtained for 9Al-10Mn-1C at 600 °C, with κ-carbides formed by discontinuous precipitation, narrow inter-lamellar spacing, and the highest hardness of 439 HV (Table 2), and the literature suggests better tensile ductility.
evidence_text: the best microstructure seems to be the one obtained for the 9Al-10Mn-1C at 600 °C, in this condition the carbides have been obtained by discontinuous precipitation and they have narrow inter-lamellar spacing. This leads the highest value of hardness (Table 2) and the literature also suggest the better tensile ductility.

claim: Discontinuous precipitation develops γ + κ-carbide fine lamellae.
evidence_text: discontinuous precipitation developed + --carbide fine lamellae

claim: Precipitation transformation produces microstructures featuring fine/coarse γ + κ-carbide lamellae and carbon-depleted γ-bands with κ-carbide nodules.
evidence_text: precipitation transformation produced microstructures featuring fine/coarse + --carbide lamellae and carbon-depleted -bands + --carbide nodules.

claim: Cellular transformation develops δ + κ lamellar structures with two morphologies: wide-interspaced thick lath-like κ-carbide strips within a ferrite matrix, and δ + κ wide lamellae with high inter-lamellar spacing that do not improve hardness.
evidence_text: Cellular transformation developed + - lamellar structures and two different morphologies were detected: wide-interspaced thick lath-like --carbide strips within a ferrite matrix and + - wide lamellae with high inter-lamellar spacing that does not affect the improvement of hardness.

claim: For the composition with 12% Al and 30% Mn, spinodal decomposition coupled with cellular transformation produces the most dense and thickest nano-sized cuboidal κ-carbides, and continuity exists between cellular transformed δ lamellae and spinodal decomposed γ + κ grains.
evidence_text: Spinodal decomposition + cellular transformation featuring the most dense and thickest nano-sized cuboidal --carbides and continuity between cellular transformed- lamellae and spinodal decomposed- + - grains for the chemical composition featuring 12% Al + 30% Mn.
